name: Amami Oshima
access: Flight from Kagoshima airport (~45 min); ferry from Kagoshima port (~11 hrs)
admission: Island free; mangrove kayak rental from ¥2,500
best_season: March–June or September–November
description: A subtropical island midway between Kyushu and Okinawa with UNESCO World Heritage rainforest, mangrove kayaking, and some of the clearest sea in Japan. Amami has its own distinct culture — different music, food, and weaving tradition (Oshima Tsumugi silk) — and far fewer foreign visitors than Okinawa.

name: Chiran Peace Museum for Kamikaze Pilots
access: Bus from Kagoshima-Chuo Station to Chiran (~1 hr), then 5 min walk
best_season: Year-round
description: The most humanizing war museum in Japan. Chiran was a major kamikaze base — 1,036 young pilots departed from here for their final missions in 1945. The museum preserves their handwritten farewell letters (many expressing love for family, not ideology), personal photographs, and the Zero fighters they flew. Profoundly moving regardless of background.

name: Chiran Samurai Residence District
access: Bus from Kagoshima-Chuo Station (~1 hr) or rental car
best_season: March–April (cherry blossoms) or year-round
description: Seven perfectly preserved samurai residences with stone-walled gardens in the "Little Kyoto of Satsuma." Each garden is a distinct masterwork of Japanese landscape design — rock arrangements, clipped hedges, and borrowed scenery using Chiran's forested mountains. The moss-covered stone walls along the approach lane are among the most photogenic in all of Japan.

name: Ibusuki Sand Baths
access: 特急 Ibusuki-no-Tamatebako from Kagoshima-Chuo (~50 min), then 10 min walk
best_season: Year-round
description: The world's only natural geothermal sand bath. Attendants bury you in volcanic black sand heated by underground hot springs to 50°C as the ocean laps a few metres away.

name: Iso Nagisa Foot Onsen
access: City bus #1 from Kagoshima-Chuo Station (~25 min)
best_season: Year-round
description: A free outdoor foot bath set directly in volcanic lava rock on the shore of Kagoshima Bay, just steps from Sengan-en Garden. The naturally hot spring water flows through channels carved into the ancient lava, and you sit with your feet in the water looking directly at Sakurajima across the bay — a view and experience you will not find anywhere else.

name: Kaimondake Volcano
access: JR Ibusuki-Makurazaki Line to Kaimon Station (from Kagoshima-Chuo ~1 hr), then 30 min walk to trailhead
best_season: October–May (avoiding summer heat and typhoons)
description: A near-perfect conical stratovolcano nicknamed "Satsuma Fuji," rising 924m from the Satsuma Peninsula coast. The 3-hour circular hiking trail offers panoramic views of the Satsuma Peninsula, distant Yakushima and Kuchierabu islands, and Ibusuki's sand bath coast — one of the finest volcano summits in Kyushu.

name: Kirishima Jingu Shrine
access: Bus from JR Kirishima-Jingu Station (~20 min)
best_season: November–December (autumn leaves) or spring
description: One of Kyushu's oldest and most powerful shrines, dedicated to Ninigi-no-Mikoto who descended from heaven to begin the divine lineage of Japanese emperors. Set at 600m altitude in misty volcanic highlands.

name: Sakurajima
access: 15 min ferry from Kagoshima port (¥200, runs every 15 min)
best_season: Year-round (best views on clear winter days)
description: Japan's most active volcano — a brooding island connected to the mainland by a 1914 lava flow. It erupts hundreds of times a year, dusting Kagoshima city with ash and producing the world's largest radishes.

name: Sengan-en Garden & Shoko Shuseikan
access: City bus #1 from Kagoshima-Chuo Station (~25 min)
best_season: Year-round, spring plum blossoms are spectacular
description: A stunning 17th-century samurai garden set against Sakurajima volcano with Kagoshima Bay as its "borrowed scenery." Adjacent to Japan's first Western-style industrial factory complex (now a museum).

name: Shiroyama Observatory
access: City bus #1 from Kagoshima-Chuo Station (~20 min) or 40 min walk
best_season: Year-round; best at sunrise or sunset
description: A forested hilltop rising 107m behind Kagoshima city center — the site of the last stand of the Satsuma Rebellion in 1877, where Saigo Takamori died. Today the observation deck gives the city's definitive view: the entire bay, the active Sakurajima volcano perfectly framed, and on clear days, the Kirishima mountain range beyond.

name: Tenmonkan Food District
access: 10 min walk from Kagoshima-Chuo Station or tram to Tenmonkan-dori stop
best_season: Year-round
description: Kagoshima's central covered shopping arcade and the heart of its food culture. This is where to find all of the prefecture's signature dishes: shirokuma (polar bear) shaved ice, Berkshire black pork (kurobuta) shabu-shabu, chicken-sashimi (tori no tataki), and shochu distilleries offering samples. The covered arcade stays active until midnight.

name: Yakushima Island
access: Ferry from Kagoshima Takachiho Port: high-speed jet foil ~2 hrs (¥8,900) or overnight car ferry (~10 hrs)
admission: Island free; Jomon Sugi trail ¥1,000 trail maintenance fee
best_season: March–June or September–November (avoiding summer typhoons)
description: A UNESCO World Heritage island of ancient cedar forests where trees live for thousands of years. The iconic Jomon Sugi cedar — estimated at 2,170 to 7,200 years old — is the oldest living thing in Japan. About 35% of the island is national park, with more rainfall than almost anywhere else in Japan feeding waterfalls that cascade through mossy old-growth.

name: Ama-no-Iwato Shrine & Amanoyasukawara
access: 15 min by car from Takachiho town center
best_season: Year-round; autumn morning mist adds mystical atmosphere
description: The sacred cave where sun goddess Amaterasu hid, plunging the world into darkness — and the vast riverbed cavern where 8 million deities gathered to coax her back out. Today the cavern is lined with thousands of stone cairns stacked by visitors, creating an atmosphere unlike anywhere else in Japan.

name: Aoshima Island & Devil's Washboard
access: 30 min by train from Miyazaki Station
best_season: Subtropical — warm year-round; avoid typhoon season (Aug-Sep)
description: A tiny jungle-covered island connected to the mainland by a 300m bridge, surrounded by 'Devil's Washboard' — perfectly rippled rock formations shaped by millennia of ocean waves.

name: Aya Teruhane Suspension Bridge
access: 40 min by car from Miyazaki City
best_season: Autumn (late October–November) for spectacular canopy color
description: A 250m suspension bridge spanning a ravine above one of Asia's last intact broadleaf evergreen forests — ancient trees up to 800 years old in a UNESCO Biosphere Reserve. In autumn the canopy below turns gold and crimson while you walk across.

name: Cape Toi Wild Horses
access: 2 hrs by car from Miyazaki Station (no public transport)
best_season: Spring foals (April–May); Autumn golden grass
description: Japan's only herd of truly wild horses roams the dramatic cliffs and grasslands of this southern cape. Walk among them at sunrise when morning mist rolls in from Osumi Strait.

name: Ebino Plateau
access: 90 min by car from Miyazaki Station; 30 min from Kobayashi IC
best_season: Late May–June (azaleas); Autumn for clear skies and fall colors
description: A volcanic highland at 1,200m in the Kirishima range with a chain of emerald and cobalt crater lakes, and Japan's most spectacular azalea blooms in late spring. The lake circuit trail passes three different-colored volcanic lakes within a 2-hour walk.

name: Obi Castle Town
access: 10 min by bus from Obi Station (JR Nichinan Line)
best_season: Spring (cherry blossoms on the castle grounds) and Autumn
description: The most intact feudal castle town in Kyushu — locals call it the "Little Kyoto of Kyushu." Samurai estates, merchant quarters, and a pine-lined castle approach are preserved in a town that has barely changed since the Edo period.

name: Sun Messe Nichinan (Moai Statues)
access: 60 min by bus from Miyazaki Station; 30 min by car
best_season: Year-round; Spring for blue skies and clear Pacific views
description: The only place outside Easter Island with official full-size moai replica statues — seven figures overlooking the Pacific, authorized by the Rapa Nui Council. Each moai represents a different area of good fortune and can be touched for blessings.

name: Takachiho Gorge
access: 90 min by express bus from Kumamoto
best_season: Spring (April–May) and Autumn (October–November)
description: Rent a rowboat and drift through a canyon carved by the Gokase River, under towering basalt walls and a waterfall that drops directly into the gorge. Go at 8am before the crowds arrive.

name: Takachiho Shrine Night Kagura
access: Inside Takachiho Shrine
best_season: Year-round (every night at 8pm)
description: Watch masked priests perform ancient Shinto kagura dances telling the myth of Amaterasu emerging from a cave — every night at 8pm, year-round. One of Japan's most atmospheric cultural rituals.

name: Udo Jingu Shrine (Sea Cave)
access: 90 min by bus from Miyazaki Station
best_season: Year-round; dramatic in winter storms
description: A Shinto shrine built entirely inside a sea cave on a cliff above crashing Pacific waves. Access via steep stone steps carved into the cliff face. Buy mudball offerings and throw them into a rock.

name: Umagase Cliffs, Hyuga Cape
access: 15 min by car from Hyuga Station (JR Nippo Line)
best_season: Clear weather year-round; dramatically stormy in winter
description: Sheer 70m basalt columns plunge straight into the Pacific along the most dramatic stretch of Japan's eastern coastline. A glass-floor observation deck lets you stand over the void above crashing waves — and the horizon here is uninterrupted ocean all the way to the Americas.

name: Umisachi Yamasachi Sightseeing Train
access: Departs Miyazaki Station (reservation required)
best_season: Spring and Autumn for clear skies
description: A retro sightseeing train that hugs the Pacific coastline south from Miyazaki City. The ocean is so close you can see waves breaking meters away. Local obento included on some services.

name: Beppu Jigoku (Hell) Tour
access: 5 min by bus from Beppu Station
best_season: Year-round; misty winter is most atmospheric
description: Eight 'hells' of boiling springs in otherworldly colors: blood red, cobalt blue, milk white. The Umi Jigoku (Sea Hell) is strangely beautiful — and you can eat eggs boiled in its steam.

name: Harajiri Falls
access: 90 min by car from Oita Station
best_season: Rainy season (June–July) for max water; Spring and Autumn for clear air
description: Japan's widest waterfall — a 120m curtain of water dropping 20m over hexagonal basalt columns shaped by ancient Aso volcanic flows. Called "East Asia's Niagara," it's most spectacular after heavy rain when the entire rock face disappears under white water.

name: Kokonoe "Yume" Grand Suspension Bridge
access: 60 min by car from Yufu IC (Oita Expressway)
best_season: Autumn (late October–November) for spectacular foliage
description: Japan's highest pedestrian suspension bridge hangs 173m above a forested river gorge. Walk the 390m span as the canyon drops away beneath you. In autumn, the gorge becomes a blazing sea of red and orange maples — visible from both ends of the bridge.

name: Kuju Highlands (Hana Mitate)
access: 90 min by car from Oita Station
best_season: Spring (April–May) for azaleas; Autumn (Oct) for susuki
description: Japan's largest highland wildflower meadow. In late summer, millions of susuki silver grass plumes sway across volcanic hills. In spring, millions of wild azaleas paint the slopes pink.

name: Kunisaki Peninsula Buddhist Trail
access: 45 min by car from Oita Airport
best_season: Spring and Autumn; February for the fire ritual
description: A circular peninsula where a unique fusion of Buddhism and Shinto flourished 1,300 years ago. Ancient cliff temples, stone-carved kannon statues on forest paths, and fire-walking rituals that survive unchanged to this day — and almost no foreign visitors.

name: Oka Castle Ruins
access: 90 min by car from Oita Station; 5 min from Bungo-Taketa Station
best_season: Spring (cherry blossoms) and Autumn (morning fog)
description: A 12th-century mountain fortress perched on sheer cliffs above a river gorge — the place where composer Rentaro Taki played as a child and found inspiration for "Kojo no Tsuki," one of Japan's best-loved songs. Almost no foreign tourists ever come here.

name: Takasakiyama Monkey Mountain
access: 10 min by bus from Oita Station
best_season: Winter (babies born Dec–Feb) and Spring
description: Over 1,500 wild Japanese macaques roam free on this mountain above Beppu Bay. You walk among them on a mountain path — they ignore you completely. Babies cling to their mothers all winter.

name: Usa Jingu Shrine
access: 30 min by JR Nippo Line from Oita Station
best_season: Spring festival (March) and Autumn festival (October)
description: Japan's first and most important Hachiman shrine, founded in 725 AD. The original model for 40,000 Hachiman shrines across Japan. Visit on a weekday to have it almost to yourself.

name: Usuki Stone Buddhas
access: 30 min by car from Usuki Station (JR Nippo Line)
best_season: Spring and Autumn; early morning for soft light and solitude
description: Sixty Buddhist carvings emerge from a sheer mossy rock face in a cedar forest clearing — Japan's only National Treasure-designated stone carvings. Made over 1,000 years ago, the identity of their creators and their original purpose remain a mystery.

name: Yabakei Gorge
access: 30 min by car from Nakatsu IC; limited bus service
best_season: Autumn (late October–November) for peak foliage
description: One of Japan's three great scenic landscapes — a 36km corridor of volcanic tuff pillars, bizarre pinnacles, and dense hardwood forest. Almost unknown outside Japan, yet the autumn foliage here rivals anything in Kyoto.

name: Yufuin Morning Mist Valley
access: 2.5 hrs from Fukuoka by direct Yufuin no Mori train
best_season: Autumn and Winter for the densest mist
description: Arrive before 9am to catch the valley floor wrapped in thermal mist rising from the earth. No photo filter needed. Then wander Yufuin's art galleries and cheese shops.

name: Yufuin Yunohira-kaido Street
access: 5 min walk from Yufuin Station
best_season: Year-round; Autumn for foliage while strolling
description: Yufuin's main pedestrian street, lined with farm-to-table cafes, local cheese shops, craft workshops, and a Cotswolds-inspired Floral Village. The best place in Kyushu to eat wagyu beef, drink pour-over coffee, and browse Japanese craft goods at your own pace.
